coredata.dc:title: Sustainable large-scale synthesis of biologically active sulfur quantum dots using sublimed sulfur
coredata.dc:creator: Priyadarshi, R.
Riahi, Z.
Tammina, S.K.
Khan, A.
Rhim, J.-W.
coredata.dc:description: The large-scale synthesis of ethylenediamine-capped sulfur quantum dots was performed using a sustainable method that utilizes sublimated sulfur waste as a sulfur precursor and recycles ∼70% of the solvent and >50% of the water used in the process. This process achieved a high conversion efficiency of 21% and a high photoluminescence quantum yield of 79.8%, one of the highest reported, involving a reaction volume at least five times larger than previously reported methods. The SQDs exhibited biological activity with low cytotoxicity, high antioxidant and antibacterial properties, and significant anticancer activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SQD
High-value use of sulfur
Sustainable method
Scale-up
Solvent recycling
Green chemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of SQDs. (a,c) TEM images, (b) corresponding particle size distribution histogram, (d) X-ray diffraction pattern, (e, f) PL spectra showing excitation-dependent emission.

(a) XPS surface spectrum and (f) XPS high-resolution spectra of S2p showing various states of sulfur. (c) FTIR spectra of SQDs displaying characteristic bands of sulfur (highlighted in blue), EDA (highlighted in green), and interaction between EDA and sulfur (highlighted in orange). (d) Viability of mouse fibroblast L929 cells when exposed to varying SQD concentrations, indicating non-toxicity of SQDs below 500 μg/mL concentration. Antioxidant activity of SQDs in terms of (e) ABTS and (f) DPPH free radical scavenging potential.
Various methods to synthesize SQDs from sublimated sulfur and PLQY values.

row.entry: Combined ultrasound -microwave treatment with hydrogen peroxide etching
Hydrogen peroxide etching method with PSS capping
Oxygen accelerated synthesis method with CMC capping

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Antimicrobial activity of SQDs against various bacterial strains.

head.ce:title: Sustainable large-scale synthesis of biologically active sulfur quantum dots using sublimed sulfur

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The large-scale synthesis of ethylenediamine-capped sulfur quantum dots was performed using a sustainable method that utilizes sublimated sulfur waste as a sulfur precursor and recycles ∼70% of the solvent and >50% of the water used in the process. This process achieved a high conversion efficiency of 21% and a high photoluminescence quantum yield of 79.8%, one of the highest reported, involving a reaction volume at least five times larger than previously reported methods. The SQDs exhibited biological activity with low cytotoxicity, high antioxidant and antibacterial properties, and significant anticancer activity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots have received considerable attention in recent years due to their potential applications in diverse fields such as optoelectronics [1–3], energy conversion and storage [4,5], bioimaging [2,6], and food packaging [7]. Most applications have traditionally utilized organic or heavy metal-based quantum dots such as CdTe, CdSe, and CdS due to their excellent optical and luminescent properties and high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY). Despite their high PLQY, these conventional fluorophores are considered unsuitable for biological and human contact applications due to their cytotoxic potential [8,9]. On the other hand, low atomic weight quantum dots, such as carbon and sulfur quantum dots are economical [10,11], sustainable [7,12], safe and non-toxic [7,12]. Several studies also suggest their successful utilization in human contact applications [6,13–15].

In particular, sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) are a recent addition to the quantum dot family as promising functional materials for various applications [7,16,17]. It can also be easily and economically synthesized from sublimated sulfur, a by-product of the petrochemical industry [7,11]. However, most reported SQD synthesis methods are not environmentally friendly and rely on expensive and hazardous chemicals [7,16].

The first reported method for synthesizing SQDs from sublimated sulfur was the assembly fission method by Shen et al. [18]. However, this method suffered from a long reaction time (∼100 h), producing SQDs with only 3.8% PLQY, which is too low compared to the conventionally used fluorophores such as CdTe, CdSe, and organic dyes, which normally exceed 50% of PLQY [16]. Subsequently, several methods have been developed, and most were the modified versions of the assembly fission method. Some researchers have used external energy in the form of pressure [19], temperature [19,20], ultrasound [21], and microwave radiations [22] to reduce the reaction time, while others focused on surface modification of SQDs, using oxygen [23], copper ions [24], hydrogen peroxide [25] to enhance the PLQY. They achieved a PLQY of >50%, but the resulting reactions were too complex and unsuitable for industrial scale-up. Product purification is a major challenge since all these methods use highly concentrated sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solutions to dissolve elemental sulfur powder [7,16,17]. Several researchers have reported the removal of Na salts and residues using a dialysis method [7], which is much more expensive for large-scale synthesis.

Recently, Gao et al. replaced NaOH solution with ethylenediamine (EDA), used as a solvent and capping agent for sulfur [20]. Using a hydrothermal reactor, they fabricated SQDs in 5 h at 170 °C. The advantage of the method using EDA was that the solvent could be removed by simple vacuum distillation after the reaction. Additionally, they used an ethanol-mediated precipitation method to purify and collect SQDs. The PLQY reported for this process was 87.5%, the highest ever reported. Nonetheless, the hydrothermal synthesis involves high pressures, an extremely energy-intensive process unsuitable for industrial scale-up. Additionally, using ethanol for purification also increases production cost.

Based on the method of Gao et al. [20], this study attempted large-scale synthesis of SQDs using simple reflux assembly at 180 °C. We also used water instead of ethanol to resuspend the SQDs after EDA removal. The resulting SQD exhibited a high PLQY of 79.8%, one of the highest values of PLQY obtained to date for SQD production (Table 1 ). Additionally, the conversion efficiency was ∼21%, higher than the 15% of Gao et al. [20]. This reaction was performed using five times more reactant volume than the hydrothermal reactor synthesis method, proving that this large-scale synthesis method is economically reproducible at the industrial level.

To synthesize SQDs, 20 g of elemental sulfur was dissolved in 200 mL EDA with stirring at 500 rpm to obtain a dark brown liquid. After the reaction was continued for 1 h, the mixture was transferred to a round bottom flask equipped with a reflux device. While stirring vigorously at 1000 rpm, the temperature of the reaction mixture was raised to 180 °C. After the reflux reaction was continued for 6 h, the mixture was cooled to room temperature, and stirring was continued for 15 h. Excess EDA solvent was removed from the mixture by rotary evaporation, recovering approximately 70% of the solvent. The concentrated orange-brown mixture remaining in the flask was diluted with 200 mL distilled water and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm (12,298×g) for 15 min. After centrifugation, the supernatant was collected and concentrated with a rotary evaporator to recover about 50–60% of the used water. Further, the concentrate was lyophilized under a vacuum at −80 °C to obtain SQDs. The synthesis reaction takes place as follows:
(1) 2H2NCH2CH2NH2 + Sx → (H2NCH2CH2NH3 +) (H2NCH2CH2NH-Sx –)
(2) (H2NCH2CH2NH3 +) (H2NCH2CH2NH-Sx –) + H+ → 2(H2NCH2CH2NH3 +) + Sx ↓

The synthesis mechanism involves the dissolution of sulfur in EDA to produce open-chain alkylammonium polysulfides (Eq. (1)) and reacts with H+ to regenerate zero-valent sulfur passivated by EDA (Eq. (2)) to form SQDs. The H+ ions were provided by the H2S gas produced during this reaction. The method does not involve high-pressure conditions, commonly used for the hydrothermal synthesis of quantum dots, including SQDs, where a stainless-steel hydrothermal autoclave is used. In addition, the two solvents used in this reaction (EDA and water) are recovered and recycled, making it a sustainable and environmentally friendly process. The steps followed during SQD synthesis are shown in Figure S1, Electronic Supporting Information (ESI). The synthesized SQDs were characterized to ascertain their morphology and size via transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and the chemical structure was determined using photoluminescence (PL) spectrophotometry, X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The details of the characterization methods have been provided in the Electronic Supporting Information (ESI).

The particles' morphological analysis was conducted using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and the image is shown in Fig. 1 a,c. The shape of the SQDs was quasi-spherical, similar to that observed by other researchers who prepared SQDs using various other synthesis methods [17]. The size of the SQDs ranged from 1 nm to 10 nm, most of which fell within the 3–5 nm size range, with an average particle size of 4.9 nm (Fig. 1b). This 1–10 nm size range refers to the quantum range where the quantum confinement effect occurs [5], and the particles exhibit absorption-dependent emission characteristics. The XRD pattern (Fig. 1d) confirmed that the structure of the synthesized SQDs corresponded to the JCPDS No. 08–0247. The major diffraction peaks were obtained at 23.5° and 29.1°, corresponding to the characteristic (222) and (040) lattice planes of sulfur [20,32,33]. Other researchers have also confirmed similar sulfur structures in SQDs [20]. Photoluminescence spectrophotometry confirmed the excitation-dependent emission property of SQDs (Fig. 1e), a characteristic of quantum dots. The SQDs displayed a maximum emission wavelength of 439 nm when excited at 357 nm (Fig. 1f).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted to obtain information on the chemical composition and valance states of SQDs. The survey spectrum of SQDs (Fig. 2 a) displayed binding energy peaks corresponding to O1s, N1s, C1s, and S2p, confirming the presence of oxygen, nitrogen, carbon, and sulfur. The presence of nitrogen and carbon suggests EDA successfully capping SQDs, while oxygen indicates the oxidized form of sulfur in the SQD core structure [20,34]. Further confirmation was obtained through high-resolution XPS spectral analysis of sulfur (S2p), as shown in Fig. 2b. The spectrum revealed binding energy peaks at 160.9 eV, 162.1 eV, and 162.5 eV, corresponding to the zero-valent or atomic state of sulfur (S0). In addition, peaks at binding energies >166 eV, specifically at 166.9 eV and 168.1 eV, were observed, corresponding to SO3 2− (2p2/3) and SO2 2− (2p2/3), respectively, confirming the presence of the oxidized form of sulfur in SQDs [35].

The zeta potential analysis was performed to determine the surface charge of the particles in the aqueous solution. A slightly negative zeta potential value of −4.97 mV was observed for the SQDs (Figure S2, ESI), indicating relatively low stability and a high tendency to aggregate at high concentrations [36]. This was also evident when the visible emission under UV light decreased at high SQD concentrations, and the concentrations 1200 μg/mL and below displayed high fluorescence intensities (Figure S3, ESI).

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometry was conducted to confirm further the chemical structure of SQDs (Fig. 2c). The spectrum displayed characteristic bands of SQDs and EDA, a capping and stabilizing agent of SQDs. The absorption bands at 3293 cm−1 and 3168 cm−1 corresponded to asymmetric and symmetric N–H stretching vibrations, respectively, of the amine group in EDA [37]. The sharp bands around 2866 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–H bonds in EDA [37,38]. The absorption band at 1652 cm−1 indicates N–H bending vibrations, while the band at 1360 cm−1 corresponds to EDA's aliphatic C–H bending vibrations [38,39]. In addition to the characteristic bands of the EDA capping, the spectra also exhibited the characteristic absorption bands of SQDs. The band corresponding to SO vibrations was observed at 1527 cm−1 [34], and bands at 653 cm−1 and 529 cm−1 corresponded to S–O stretching and S–S stretching vibrations, respectively [34,38]. These characteristic bands of SQDs indicate the presence of both zero-valent and oxidized forms of sulfur in the SQDs. Further, the absorption bands at 1080 cm−1 and 755 cm−1 are attributed to S–N–S and S–N stretching vibrations, respectively, indicating the chemical interaction between the capping agent and the SQDs [34].

The safety and non-toxicity of SQDs are paramount for use in biological and human contact applications. The MTT assay evaluated the cytotoxicity of the synthesized SQDs towards L929 mouse fibroblast cells, and the results are shown in Fig. 2d. When exposed to various concentrations of SQD, >80% cell viability was observed until the exposure concentration reached 500 μg/mL, after which cell viability declined rapidly. The IC50 value of SQDs for L929 cells was estimated to be 741.1 μg/mL (Figure S4, ESI). The cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of SQDs are highly dependent on the surface capping of SQDs. Surface functional groups play an important role in determining the interaction of SQDs with biological membranes and cellular structures. In a previous study, PEG-capped SQDs were safer for L929 cells up to 1000 μg/mL concentrations [38]. SQDs have also been reported to be safer than other normal cell lines. For instance, 100 μg/mL concentrations for Chang Liver cells [26], 200 μg/mL for 293 T cells [31], and 375 μg/mL for BEAS-2B cells [29] were within safe limits. Additionally, these concentrations have been reported to be much higher than those required for biological studies [17]. Therefore, the 500 μg/mL exposure level observed in this study is significantly higher than the concentration used in biological studies.

The antioxidant activity of the SQDs was studied against 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) and 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) oxidative free radicals, simulating the aqueous and organic media, respectively. An exposure concentration of 75 μg/mL was enough to completely scavenge ABTS free radicals (Fig. 2e), while an exposure concentration of 100 μg/mL could scavenge ∼66% DPPH free radicals (Fig. 2f). Moreover, the antioxidant potential of SQD is similar to that of ascorbic acid, an antioxidant commonly used in biological systems (Figure S5, ESI). In particular, the outstanding ability of SQDs to scavenge oxidative free radicals in aqueous media, a common component of biological systems, indicates the potential antioxidant activity of SQDs in biological and related systems.

In addition, the antibacterial activity of SQD against four common pathogens, including Gram-negative E. coli and S. enterica and Gram-positive B. cereus and L. monocytogenes, was investigated (Table 2 ). The SQDs showed a much lower minimum inhibition concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) against Gram-positive bacteria compared to the Gram-negative ones, similar to those reported for SQDs [40], and is a characteristic of sulfur [11]. The MIC/MBC values obtained against B. cereus and L. monocytogenes were 75/150 μg/mL and 50/75 μg/mL, respectively. For both the biological properties, i.e., antioxidant and antimicrobial activities, the effective concentration of the SQDs used was much below their safe concentration of 500 μg/mL. Apart from antimicrobial, the anticancer properties of the synthesized SQDs were also determined against breast cancer cell lines T-47D and Hs578T (Figure S6, ESI). The IC50 values of the SQDs against these cells were 588.5 μg/mL and 455.4 μg/mL, respectively. Also, for anticancer tests, the effective concentration of SQDs was much lower than the IC50 value of 741.1 μg/mL obtained towards L929 cells to determine the safety of SQDs.

Sulfur quantum dots were synthesized on a large scale using an industrially scalable, sustainable, and environmentally friendly method. Sublimated sulfur, a waste by-product of the petroleum industry, has been used as a sulfur precursor, and approximately 70% of the EDA used as a solvent in this process could be recycled. This method produced highly luminescent SQDs with a PLQY of 79.8%, the highest obtained to date, and a conversion efficiency of 21%. The prepared SQDs were non-toxic for human contact and biological applications up to an effective concentration of 500 μg/mL with an IC50 value of 741.1 μg/mL. They were biologically active with a much lower effective concentration, exhibiting antioxidant activity at 100 μg/mL and antimicrobial activity against Gram-positive B. cereus and L. monocytogenes bacteria at <150 μg/mL. Further, the SQDs also effectively reduced the growth of breast cancer cell lines T-47D (IC50 = 588.5 μg/mL) and Hs578T (IC50 = 455.4 μg/mL) in preliminary studies. Therefore, this method is innovative for preparing sustainable SQDs with low toxicity, high PLQY, better yield, and significant biological activity.
Ruchir Priyadarshi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Visualization, Writing original draft, review and editing.
Zohreh Riahi: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Visualization.
Sai Kumar Tammina: Investigation, Data curation.
Ajahar Khan: Investigation, Data curation.
Jong-Whan Rhim: Review and editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Brain Pool Program funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT, and Future Planning through the National Research Foundation of Korea (RS-2023-00218228) and the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2022R1A2B5B02001422).
